coredata.dc:title: Curcumin-derived carbon quantum dots: Dual actions in mitigating tau hyperphosphorylation and amyloid beta aggregation
coredata.dc:creator: Lim, Jie Lay
Lin, Chin-Jung
Huang, Chih-Ching
Chang, Lin-Chau
coredata.dc:description: The amyloid cascade and tau hypotheses both hold significant implications for the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Curcumin shows potential by inhibiting the aggregation of amyloid beta (Aβ) and reducing tau hyperphosphorylation, however, its use is limited due to issues with solubility and bioavailability. Carbon dots, recognized for their high biocompatibility and optimal water solubility, have demonstrated the capability to inhibit either Aβ or tau aggregation. Nonetheless, their effects on tau hyperphosphorylation are yet to be extensively explored. This study aims to evaluate the water-soluble curcumin-derived carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs) synthesized via an eco-friendly method, designed to preserve the beneficial effects of curcumin while overcoming solubility challenges. The synthesis of Cur-CQDs involves a single-step dry heating process using curcumin, resulting in dots that exhibit negligible cytotoxicity to SH-SY5Y cells at the examined concentrations. Notably, Cur-CQDs have shown the ability to simultaneously mitigate Aβ aggregation and tau hyperphosphorylation. Therefore, it is suggested that Cur-CQDs may hold potential for AD treatment, a hypothesis deserving of further research.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of β-amyloid (1−42) monomers and fibrils
Cur-CQDs significantly inhibited the aggregation of Aβ42 monomers
Cur-CQDs suppressed hyperphosphorylation of tau protein

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Cur-CQDs. (a) Schematic representation of the preparation and formation mechanism of Cur-CQDs through mild pyrolysis. (b) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and (c) high resolution TEM images of as-prepared Cur-CQDs. (d) The photographs of curcumin and Cur-CQDs and their corresponding aqueous solutions.

Inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation at 37 oC. (a) Dot blot assays showing the formation of Aβ42 fibrils (recognized by the OC antibody) from Aβ42 monomers (25 µM) in the absence or presence of various concentrations of Cur-CQDs. Within (a), subfigures (i) to (iv) represent the effects of Cur-CQDs pyrolyzed at different temperatures. The upper image on each subfigure, (a,i) to (a,iv), displays the representative results from dot blot assays. The column chart below the representative image presents the mean ± standard deviation of three independent experiments of the dot blot assays. *, p < 0.05, ***, p < 0.001, ****, p < 0.0001 (as compared to the control group, 0 µg mL−1). (b,i) The aggregation kinetics and (b,ii) the maximum increase of fluorescence intensities of Aβ42 monomers (25 µM) in the absence or presence of 100 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs, AC-CQDs, MAN-CQDs, and curcumin evaluated with thioflavin T assay. ###, p < 0.001, ####, p < 0.0001 (as compared to the control group). **, p < 0.01, ****, p < 0.0001 (as compared to the AC-CQDs group). ■, p < 0.05, ■■■, p < 0.001, ■■■■, p < 0.0001 (as compared to the MAN-CQDs group). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of three independent experiments.

Effects of various concentrations of (a,i) Cur-CQDs-180 and (a,ii) Cur-CQDs-210 on inhibiting Aβ42 aggregation. (b) The lag time of Aβ42 aggregation in the absence or presence of Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210. **, p < 0.01 (as compared to 0 µg mL⁻¹ of Cur-CQDs-210). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of three independent experiments. (c) The percentage of the inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation and the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of Cur-CQDs-180 or Cur-CQDs-210.

(a) Cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells treated with or without various concentrations of Cur-CQDs or curcumin. **, p < 0.01, ****, p < 0.0001 (as compared to curcumin control group). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of three independent experiments. Expression levels of (b) phospho-tau (p-tau S262) and (c) total tau (tau 46) proteins of SH-SY5Y cells treated with 100 nM of okadaic acid (OA) for 3 h after pre-treatment with or without 100 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs for 24 h. *, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01 (as compared to OA only group). #, p < 0.05, ##, p < 0.01 (as compared to the control group). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of three independent experiments. (d) Expression levels of phospho-tau (p-tau S262) proteins relative to total tau (tau 46) proteins of SH-SY5Y cells. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of three independent experiments. *, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01 (as compared to OA only group).

head.ce:title: Curcumin-derived carbon quantum dots: Dual actions in mitigating tau hyperphosphorylation and amyloid beta aggregation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: 33 Linsen S. Rd., Zhongzheng Dist., Taipei City 100025, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amyloid cascade and tau hypotheses both hold significant implications for the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Curcumin shows potential by inhibiting the aggregation of amyloid beta (Aβ) and reducing tau hyperphosphorylation, however, its use is limited due to issues with solubility and bioavailability. Carbon dots, recognized for their high biocompatibility and optimal water solubility, have demonstrated the capability to inhibit either Aβ or tau aggregation. Nonetheless, their effects on tau hyperphosphorylation are yet to be extensively explored. This study aims to evaluate the water-soluble curcumin-derived carbon quantum dots (Cur-CQDs) synthesized via an eco-friendly method, designed to preserve the beneficial effects of curcumin while overcoming solubility challenges. The synthesis of Cur-CQDs involves a single-step dry heating process using curcumin, resulting in dots that exhibit negligible cytotoxicity to SH-SY5Y cells at the examined concentrations. Notably, Cur-CQDs have shown the ability to simultaneously mitigate Aβ aggregation and tau hyperphosphorylation. Therefore, it is suggested that Cur-CQDs may hold potential for AD treatment, a hypothesis deserving of further research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data associated with this study are present in the paper or the Supplementary Materials. All raw data are available upon request by Email to: linchauchang@ntu.edu.tw (L.-C. Chang).

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a neurological condition marked by a subtle onset and progressive decline in mental and behavioral skills. Current intervention for AD is mainly symptomatic treatments such as acetylcholine esterase (AChE) inhibitors and N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) antagonists. Recently, two drugs (aducanumab and lecanemab-irmb) aimed at the reduction of amyloid beta plaques were approved by the Food and Drug Administration in the United States under an accelerated approval pathway for which verification of clinical benefits in confirmatory trials are required [1,2]. Hence a safe and more effective therapeutic agent is needed since the mechanisms of AD are still in a mist, and the drugs in current clinical use are limited. The amyloid cascade and the protein tau hyperphosphorylation are two common hypotheses depicting the pathological causes of AD.

According to the amyloid cascade hypothesis, the abnormally accumulated amyloid beta (Aβ) plaques in the brain mainly consisted of Aβ peptides [3]. These peptides containing 39–44 amino acids were produced through the consecutive cleavage of amyloid precursor protein (APP) by β-secretase and γ-secretase [4]. Aβ40 is the more abundant form, accounting for about 90% of Aβ production in the brain, while Aβ42 makes up about 10%. Studies have shown that Aβ42 was more likely to aggregate into toxic oligomers and fibrils than Aβ40 [5,6]. Furthermore, Aβ42 has been shown to be more strongly associated with cognitive decline and AD pathology than Aβ40 [7]. In addition, mutations in the APP gene that increased the production of Aβ42 were associated with familial forms of AD [8]. Overall, while both Aβ40 and Aβ42 were involved in the pathogenesis of AD, Aβ42 was considered to be more toxic and pathogenic than Aβ40.

The tau protein normally plays an important role in stabilizing microtubules in neurons, which are important for maintaining the structure of neurons and facilitating intracellular transport. The tau hypothesis proposed that abnormal phosphorylation of the tau protein, a major component of the neurofibrillary tangles found in the brains of individuals with AD and other neurodegenerative disorders, led to the formation of insoluble aggregates of tau proteins known as neurofibrillary tangles [9]. These tangles disrupted the normal functioning of neurons and eventually led to their death, resulting in the progressive cognitive impairment observed in AD. Both Aβ and hyperphosphorylated tau contributed to synaptic abnormalities in AD. As a result, modulating Aβ aggregation and ameliorating tau hyperphosphorylation are critical in treating AD. Studies reported the potentials of certain small molecules (e.g., methylene blue, aminothienopyridazines, rhodanines, idebenone, and memantine) [10–13], monoclonal antibodies (e.g., aducanumab, ibrutinib, and bapineuzumab) [14–16], and natural products (e.g., curcumin, epigallocatechin-3-gallate, resveratrol, and berberine) [17–20] in modulating Aβ aggregation or tau hyperphosphorylation. Still, few have been reported to exert effects through both pathways.

Heavy metal-based quantum dots (QDs) with intriguing emissive properties have long captured significant attention even prior to their recent recognition through the 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry [21]. More recently, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs), including carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), prepared by the carbonization of small molecules have attracted great attention due to easy preparation, high diversity, good water solubility, and stability [22]. Due to their small size, unique optical properties, and high biocompatibility, they have gained much attention in various biomedical research fields, including drug delivery, cancer imaging and therapy, bioimaging, and biosensing [22]. CDs have been shown to inhibit Aβ aggregation [23–25] or tau aggregation [26,27]. However, to the best of our knowledge, their effects on decreasing tau hyperphosphorylation have not been discussed in detail.

Much evidence showed that curcumin could inhibit Aβ aggregation and reduce hyperphosphorylation of tau protein [28–32], but the poor bioavailability of curcumin limited its application in medical use. The aim of the present study was to prepare water-soluble curcumin-derived CQDs (Cur-CQDs) using a green synthesis method that retained the beneficial effects of curcumin while addressing solubility issues. Cur-CQDs synthesized through a one-step dry heating process was found to exert dual effects on modulating Aβ aggregation and ameliorating tau hyperphosphorylation. In the present study, the as-prepared ammonia citrate-derived CQDs (AC-CQDs) and mannose-derived CQDs (MAN-CQDs) as controls was primarily intended to emphasize the importance of the source material, curcumin, in the synthesis of CQDs. By juxtaposing these with Cur-CQDs, we aimed to underscore the distinct characteristics and potential applications derived from this specific source material, thereby contributing to a more comprehensive understanding of the unique properties of Cur-CQDs. The influence on Aβ aggregation was evaluated by dot blot assay, thioflavin T assay, circular dichroism (CD), and sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE), while SH-SY5Y cells were used as the model cells for the investigation of tau hyperphosphorylation.

Cur-CQDs, AC-CQDs, and MAN-CQDs were synthesized and characterized as previously reported [33]. Briefly, curcumin powder (J. T. Baker, Phillipsburg, NJ, USA) was heated under 120, 150, 180, or 210 oC for 2 h. After cooling down, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) with a pH of 12 was added and the resultant solutions were sonicated for 1 h, followed by centrifugation with a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 35,000 g for 1 h. The supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) with a molecular-weight cutoff of 0.5–1.0 kDa for 5 h in sodium chloride (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) solutions. Subsequently, ultrapure water was used to replace the solution, and the dialysis process was continued for an additional 18 h. For the synthesis of AC-CQDs and MAN-CQDs, the ammonia citrate (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) or mannose (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was heated at 180 oC for 2 h, respectively. After cooling down, they were dissolved into Milli-Q ultrapure deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm; EMD Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) and sonicated for 1 h, followed by centrifugation at an RCF of 35,000 g for 1 h. The supernatant was subsequently stored in a refrigerator at 4 oC for future use. The concentrations of obtained CQDs were determined by the freeze-drying method. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Cur-CQDs were recorded using Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OG, USA) systems operating at 200 kV. Samples containing Cur-CQDs (1.0 mg mL−1) were 10 times diluted in water (50 µL) before undergoing TEM measurements. The diluted Cur-CQDs were carefully loaded on 300-mesh carbon-coated Cu grids (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA). The surplus solvent underwent evaporation at room temperature and standard atmospheric pressure. The potential constituents of the Cur-CQDs were analyzed through laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (LDI-MS). MS experiments were performed in the reflectron positive-ion mode using an AutoflexIII LDI-MS (Bruker Daltonics, Bremen, Germany). For LDI-MS measurements, aliquots (1.5 µL) of the Cur-CQDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1) were pipetted onto a stainless steel 384-well LDI target (Bruker Daltonics). Please refer to Supporting Information for experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of β-amyloid (1−42) monomers and fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: Aβ42 monomer solutions were prepared by using a procedure described by Stine et al. with a slight modification [34]. Briefly, Aβ42 lyophilized powder (AnaSpec, Fremont, CA, USA) was dissolved in 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP) (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) to a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1; the solution was then incubated at 37 oC for 1 h and sonicated for 10 min. The resultant solution was aliquoted and evaporated using a stream of nitrogen gas until completely dry. To ensure complete removal of HFIP, the solution was allowed to evaporate under a stream of nitrogen gas for an additional 10 min. The stock solution was then stored at − 20 oC for future use. For the preparation of Aβ42 monomer solution, 20 mM of sodium hydroxide (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was added, and the mixture was sonicated in an ice bath for 10 min before use. The solution was then centrifuged at 16,000 g for 20 min at 4 oC. 80% of the supernatant was collected as a stock solution of the Aβ42 monomer. To prepare the Aβ42 fibril solutions, the Aβ42 monomer solution was allowed to aggregate at 300 rpm for three days.

25 µM of Aβ42 monomers or fibrils were incubated with or without Cur-CQDs (0–100 µg mL−1). At 0 or 72 h, each mixture of 2 µL was taken out and spotted onto a 0.2 µm nitrocellulose membrane (Cytiva, Marlborough, MA, USA). After air drying, the membrane was blocked for 30 min by BlockPRO™ protein-free blocking buffer (Visual Protein, Taiwan). After that, the membrane was then directly incubated with primary antibody OC (anti-amyloid fibrils, 1:3000) (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) for 1 h at room temperature. Following three 5-minute washes with TBST (tris-buffered saline with Tween-20) (150 mM NaCl (Bioshop, Canada), 20 mM Tris (Bionavas, Canada), 0.1% Tween 20 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA)), the membrane underwent incubation with a secondary antibody labeled with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (anti-rabbit 1:3000) (Cell Signaling, Danvers, MA, USA) at room temperature for 1 h. Unbound secondary antibodies were washed off using TBST for 5 min thrice. The image was developed using an enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) kit (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). The semi-quantitation was performed by Image Lab (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA).

Thioflavin T fluorescence assay was performed by using the microplate reader (Spectramax Paradigm, Molecular Devices Corp., Sunnyvale, CA). A stock solution of 200 µL was prepared by using the PBS buffer (137 mM NaCl (Bioshop, Canada), 2.7 mM KCl (FUJIFILM Wako Chemicals, Richmond, VA, USA), 10 mM sodium phosphate dibasic (J. T. Baker, Phillipsburg, NJ, USA), 1.8 mM potassium phosphate monobasic (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA)) containing 25 µM of Aβ42 monomers or fibrils, 10 µM of thioflavin T (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), and various concentrations of CQDs or curcumin. Working solutions of 100 µL each were added to the 96-well black plate (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), and the plate was tightly sealed. The experiment was performed at 37 oC. The fluorescence intensity was measured by using an excitation wavelength at 460 nm and an emission wavelength at 500 nm for every 2.5 min with a 5 s shake prior to measurement. The fluorescence intensity was then fitted using the following equation: [23,35].

For calculation of the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210, 25 µM of Aβ42 monomer solutions were incubated with a serial dilution of Cur-CQDs-180 or Cur-CQDs-210 with a dilution factor of 2. The fluorescence intensity was recorded and fitted using the equation described above. The calculation of IC50 was conducted [36]. Briefly, the log concentration and its inhibitory effect were plotted and fitted using a 4-parameter logistic model, which may be expressed as:
Y =
a − d
1 +
X
c
b
+ d
where Y is the response and X is the concentration. a is the bottom of the curve (lower plateau) and d is the top of the curve (upper plateau). b is the slope factor of the curve at the linear portion. c is the concentration needed to reach 50% of the upper plateau, which is the IC50.

25 µM of Aβ42 monomers/fibrils were incubated with or without 100 µg mL−1 of CQDs or curcumin for 72 h. At 0 h and 72 h, samples were mixed with Laemmli sample buffer (31.5 mM tris-HCl, pH 6.8 (Bionavas, Canada), 10% glycerol (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), 1% SDS (SERVA Electrophoresis GmbH, Germany), 0.005% bromophenol blue (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA)) without heating. Samples were then added to 4–20% SDS-PAGE gradient gel (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) and then run at 90 V for 2 h. After 2 h, the gel was transferred into a 0.45 µm PVDF membrane (Cytiva, Marlborough, MA, USA) at 100 V for 1 h. The membrane was then blocked with 1% bovine serum albumin (BSA) (Gibco, Waltham, MA, USA) for 60 min. After that, the membrane was washed three times for 15 min with TBST, and then incubated with primary antibody OC (anti-amyloid fibrils, 1:3000) for 1 h at room temperature. After being washed three times with TBST for 5 min, the membrane was then incubated with horseradish peroxidase-labeled secondary antibody (anti-rabbit 1:3000) at room temperature for 1 h. Unbound secondary antibodies were washed off using TBST for 15 min thrice. The detection was performed using an ECL chemiluminescent kit.

The secondary structures of Aβ42 monomers and fibrils were investigated using circular dichroism (J-720, Jasco, Japan). In investigating Aβ42 aggregation, 700 µL of solution containing 25 µM Aβ42 monomers was mixed with or without 100 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs prepared in PBS buffer and incubated at 37 oC under 300 rpm. At 0 h and 72 h, 300 µL of the sample solution was taken out for measurement. The ellipticity was recorded using a 1.0 mm path length quartz cell (Jasco, Japan) with a range between 200 nm and 250 nm at a scanning speed of 50 nm min−1 and a bandwidth of 1 nm. The spectra for each sample were obtained as the average of five consecutive scans.

Human neuroblastoma cells, SH-SY5Y (ATCC CRL-2266), were kindly provided by Prof. Wen-Jen Lin (School of Pharmacy, National Taiwan University). Cells were cultured in T75 flasks (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/Nutrient Mixture F-12 (1:1) (Gibco, Waltham, MA, USA) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Gibco, Waltham, MA, USA), 100 U mL−1 penicillin G sodium salt (Biological Industreis, Israel), 100 µg mL−1 streptomycin sulfate (Biological Industreis, Israel), 250 ng mL−1 amphotericin B (Biological Industreis, Israel), 4.876 g L−1 sodium bicarbonate (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) and incubated at 37 oC with 5% CO2 in air. The medium was replaced every 2–3 days, and cells were passaged upon reaching 80–90% confluence using 0.05% trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was used for the cell viability assay. A density of 1 × 104 cells/well was used for seeding cells in 96-well plates (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), followed by a 48-hour culture period. Cells were then treated with 3.125, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs for 24 h, and cell viability was determined using the CCK-8 assay according to the protocol provided by the manufacturer. Briefly, after incubation of the cells with target compounds, the medium was aspirated, and 100 µL of medium containing 10 µL of CCK-8 was added into each well. After 2 h, the absorbance at 450 nm was recorded. The experiment was repeated three times, and cell viability was quantified as a percentage relative to the control.

SH-SY5Y cells were seeded onto a 6-well plate (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) with a density of 1 × 106 cells/well and cells were allowed to attach for 48 h. Then the culture medium was aspirated and replaced with the culture medium with or without 100 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs. After 24 h of incubation, the medium was replaced with that containing 100 nM of okadaic acid (OA) (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) for 3 h. After treatment of OA for 3 h, the culture plate was placed on ice and cells were collected using a cell scraper. Cells were centrifuged at an RCF of 450 g for 5 min at 4 oC and washed using ice-cold PBS. After washing, cells were then centrifuged at an RCF of 800 g for 2 min at 4 oC, and PBS was aspirated.

Radioimmunoprecipitation assay (RIPA) lysis buffer containing 50 mM tris base, 150 mM NaCl, 1% Triton X-100 (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), 0.1% SDS, 0.5% sodium deoxycholate (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), 1% Halt™ Protease and Phosphatase Inhibitor Cocktail (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) were added into the cell pellet and placed on ice for 15 min. The cell lysates were then centrifuged at 14,000 g for 15 min at 4 oC. The supernatant was collected and heated at 95 oC for 5 min with the addition of Laemmli sample buffer (31.5 mM tris-HCl, pH 6.8, 10% glycerol, 1% SDS, 0.005% bromophenol blue). Protein samples were then stored at − 20 oC for future use. Bio-Rad protein assay kit (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) was used in protein concentration estimation. Absorption intensity at 595 nm was recorded using a microplate reader (SpectraMax Paradigm, Molecular Devices, San Jose, CA, USA), and the protein concentration was determined by using a BSA standard calibration curve.

Cell lysates containing 15 µg of proteins were loaded into a 10% polyacrylamide gel and run for 1 h at 90 V and 120 V. The gel was transferred into a 0.45 µm PVDF membrane at 100 V for 1 h. PVDF membranes were incubated with 0.1% BSA (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) for 1 h and washed 3 times for 15 min with TBST. Anti-β-actin (1:10000 diluted with 0.1% BSA with TBST) (Abcam, Cambridge, UK), anti-phospho-tau (p-tau S262) (1:5000 diluted with 0.1% BSA with TBST) (Abcam, Cambridge, UK), and anti-tau (tau 46) (1:5000 diluted with 0.1% BSA with TBST) (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) were added and incubated at 4 oC overnight with gentle agitation.

Antibodies were removed and washed 3 times for 15 min with TBST. HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies (1:5000 diluted with 0.1% BSA with TBST) were added and incubated for 1 h at room temperature with gentle agitation. After incubation, secondary antibodies were removed and washed 3 times with TBST. Finally, the images were developed using an ECL chemiluminescence kit and detected using a chemiluminescence imaging system (ChemiDocTM MP Imaging System, Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA). Semi-quantification was conducted using ImageLab Software (ChemiDocTM MP Imaging System, Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA).

Statistical analysis software (JMP®, Version 16. SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC) was used and a p value smaller than 0.05 was considered significant. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used and all pairs, Tukey HSD (honestly significant difference) post-hoc test comparisons were then performed.

Typically, CQDs were formed through dehydration, condensation, polymerization, and carbonization of carbon-based small molecules [37,38]. In the present study, Cur-CQDs were synthesized following our previous report [33]. Briefly, curcumin powder was pyrolyzed at 120, 150, 180, or 210 oC for 2 h, and subsequent alkaline (200 mM sodium phosphate buffer; pH 12.0) treatment led to the assembly of CQDs. The dehydration-mediated condensation of curcumin led to the formation of polymeric curcumin, which can further crosslink to micro-sized supramolecular structures ( Fig. 1a) [33]. Prolonged heating caused fragmentation and carbonization forming CQDs anchored with pyrolyzed curcumin moieties on their surfaces. The CQDs prepared at 120, 150, 180, or 210 oC were denoted as Cur-CQDs-120, Cur-CQDs-150, Cur-CQDs-180, and Cur-CQDs-210, and the yield was determined to be 10.21%, 15.72%, 22.33%, and 25.26%, respectively. The diameters obtained by the transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis (from 100 to 150 counts for each) for Cur-CQDs-120, Cur-CQDs-150, Cur-CQDs-180, and Cur-CQDs-210 were 21.1 ± 2.7 nm, 17.5 ± 0.2 nm, 13.6 ± 2.0 nm, and 6.9 ± 1.1 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) revealed crystalline structures with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm ascribing to the (100) and (002) plane of graphitic carbon core (Fig. 1c) [39]. Contrary to the solubility of curcumin (about 0.5 μg mL−1) in water, all Cur-CQDs showed excellent aqueous solubility (>20 mg mL−1) (Fig. 1d). The high zeta potential (ζ) of Cur-CQDs (about −25 to −35 mV) implied that abundant phenolic and carboxylic groups on the CQDs endowed their high aqueous solubilities. The detailed optical properties can be found in our previous report [33]. The LDI-MS spectra revealed that Cur-CQDs retain most of the essential moieties of curcumin, including anisole (theoretical m/z 109.06) and guaiacol (theoretical m/z 125.06) on its surfaces as depicted in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) as well as in our previous report [33], strongly supporting that the highly bioactive moieties of curcumin were preserved on the Cur-CQDs' surfaces [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cur-CQDs significantly inhibited the aggregation of Aβ42 monomers
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of Cur-CQDs to inhibit the aggregation of Aβ42 monomers was assessed through dot blot assays utilizing anti-amyloid fibrils (OC) antibodies. At the start of the incubation (t = 0 h), the initiation of Aβ42 monomer aggregation was observed, and a few resulting fibrils were detected across all groups ( Fig. 2a). After 72 h of incubation, the groups without Cur-CQDs exhibited a considerably higher fibril count compared to the groups with Cur-CQDs. Notably, at a concentration of 100 µg mL−1, Cur-CQDs showed a significant effect in decreasing fibril formation. Interestingly, Cur-CQDs-120 and Cur-CQDs-210 exhibited notable reductions in fibril formation at 1 µg mL−1. The dot blot assay results showed that Cur-CQDs could inhibit the aggregation of Aβ42 monomers. While curcumin was previously shown to inhibit fibril formation [41], our current findings suggested that curcumin retained its inhibitory properties after pyrolysis at specific temperatures.

Thioflavin T assay was also conducted since thioflavin T exhibited enhanced fluorescence upon binding to amyloid fibrils. Fig. 2b shows the aggregation kinetics of Aβ42 incubated without or with 100 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs or curcumin. The Cur-CQDs showed inhibition of fibril formation to various extents in comparison with the control group where only Aβ42 monomers were added. Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 exhibited similar inhibitory effects to curcumin, indicating that the pyrolysis of curcumin into Cur-CQDs did not diminish the inhibitory effects on the fibril formation of Aβ42 monomers. On the other hand, the CQDs prepared from ammonia citrate or mannose at 180 oC, denoted as AC-CQDs and MAN-CQDs with the sizes about 5 nm and 12 nm, respectively, showed lesser inhibitory activities compared to Cur-CQDs. In addition, Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 showed better inhibitory abilities than Cur-CQDs-120 and Cur-CQDs-150 did.

Sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) was used to investigate the degree of aggregation of Aβ42 in the absence or presence of Cur-CQDs or curcumin (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Initially, Aβ42 peptides were mainly detected at low molecular weights (below 5 kDa) (Fig. S2a), indicating that they were mostly monomers. Some bands between 11 and 17 kDa may be ascribed to the self-assembly process that formed oligomers. After 72 h of incubation, bands of higher molecular weights (35–100 kDa) were observed in lane 1 (Aβ42 only), suggesting that Aβ42 peptides were aggregated (Fig. S2b). Samples containing AC-CQDs or MAN-CQDs in addition to Aβ42 showed similar profiles as that with Aβ42 only.

While bands of higher molecular weights were observable for all samples, including Aβ42 (lane 1), samples incubated with Cur-CQDs (lanes 2, 3, 4, and 5) or curcumin (lane 6) revealed bands of relatively lower molecular weights compared to lane 1. This observation indicated the partial inhibition of Aβ42 monomer aggregation by Cur-CQDs and curcumin. Notably, samples with the addition of Cur-CQDs-120, Cur-CQDs-150, Cur-CQDs-180, and Cur-CQDs-210 demonstrated stronger bands at lower molecular weights than that with curcumin, suggesting the potentially superior inhibitory efficacy of Cur-CQDs. However, further investigations are necessary to elucidate the specific inhibitory mechanisms. Additionally, the samples containing Cur-CQDs-210 (lane 5) exhibited higher intensities of bands at lower molecular weights, providing additional evidence of the robust inhibition of fibril formation by Cur-CQDs-210, as observed in the thioflavin T assay.

We further employed CD spectrometry for investigating the inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation by Cur-CQDs (Fig. S3a, Supporting Information). While Aβ42 monomers aggregated into fibrils, the secondary structure would change from random coils into beta-sheets. Initially, the negative peak near 200 nm suggested a random coil structure of Aβ42. After 72 h of incubation, the positive ellipticity at around 200 nm and the negative ellipticity at around 215 nm implied the formation of the β-sheeted structure [42,43]. Again, Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 showed superior inhibitory activities with larger reducing extent of β-sheet formation, which was consistent with the findings from TEM images. After 72 h of incubation, Aβ42 fibrils were clearly observed from the TEM images (Fig. S3b) [44,45], whereas Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 significantly inhibited the formation of Aβ42 fibrils. Our results implied that curcumin and all Cur-CQDs investigated can inhibit Aβ fibril formation, but only Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 could effectively suppress the formation of larger fibrils. We speculated that the highly abundant anisole and guaiacol on Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 played a crucial role in the inhibition of formation of amyloid fibrils. Besides the anisole and guaiacol moieties on the surface of CQDs, the size of Cur-CQDs-180 (13.6 ± 2.0 nm) and Cur-CQDs-210 (6.9 ± 1.1 nm) were smaller than that of Cur-CQDs-120 (21.1 ± 2.7 nm) and Cur-CQDs-150 (17.5 ± 0.2 nm). The stronger inhibitory effects of smaller nanoparticles may be attributed to their higher density of active functional groups on the surface, which allowed for stronger binding [46]. We also noted that Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 had higher negative zeta potentials (about −25 mV) compared to that of Cur-CQDs-120 and Cur-CQDs-150 (about −16 mV). The present study found that nanoparticles with higher negative zeta potentials exhibited stronger inhibition of Aβ42 aggregation. This was likely because the major residues responsible for aggregation were positively charged [47,48].

Since Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 have been demonstrated potent in the inhibition of Aβ fibril formation, the dose-dependent aggregation kinetics was investigated by thioflavin T assay. Fig. 3 shows the concentration-dependent inhibition of fibril formation. The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of Cur-CQDs-180 and Cur-CQDs-210 was determined to be 16.44 ± 1.38 µg mL−1 and 23.50 ± 1.20 µg mL−1, respectively. However, the lag time (Tlag) did not significantly prolong even at a high concentration of Cur-CQDs-180 (100 μg mL−1), suggesting that Cur-CQDs-180 may not significantly affect the aggregation rate but may suppress the extent of aggregation. The results were consistent with the report that free curcumin did not significantly affect the Tlag of Aβ aggregation [49]. In contrast, Cur-CQDs-210 at a high concentration of 100 μg mL−1 significantly increased the Tlag, suggesting that Cur-CQDs-210 may increase the nucleation period of Aβ aggregation. The gel electrophoresis analysis in the presence of Cur-CQDs-210 also showed much stronger bands at low molecular weights in comparison with those of curcumin and Cur-CQDs-180. We suggested that small-sized Cur-CQDs-210 with high density of guaiacol on their surface suppressed the nucleation of Aβ42. The solid state nuclear magnetic resonance study indicated that the methoxy and hydroxy groups on the guaiacol of curcumin played a crucial role in the interaction with Aβ [50]. Our results suggested that Cur-CQDs-210 could bind to most toxic Aβ oligomers to avoid the aggregation into higher-order fibrils. The Aβ peptides were composed of three distinct regions: an unstructured N-terminus consisting of the initial 15 amino acids, a central hydrophobic cluster (CHC) encompassing residues 16–21, and a hydrophobic C-terminus containing the final 10–12 amino acids [51,52]. A recent computational analysis involving docking and subsequent multiple molecular dynamics (MD) simulations investigated the interaction between curcumin and a hexamer peptide model of the Aβ42 oligomers [52]. The study revealed that curcumin partially separated the end peptide of the Aβ42 fibril by interfering with the β-sheet structure present in the 12VHHQKLVFF20 sequence [52]. More recently, another MD simulation study showed two degraded curcumin products, ferulic aldehyde and vanillin, exhibited common interaction points along the Aβ42 sequence, specifically in the regions of 19FF20, 34LMVGG38, and 9GY10 [51]. This suggested a uniformity in the molecular recognition sites of the Aβ42 dimer across these degraded curcumin molecules. Notably, the 19FF20 and 34LMVGG38 hot spot regions were components of the CHC and the C-terminus hydrophobic region, respectively. These regions were responsible for driving the fibrillization of Aβ. In addition, the 17LVFF20 segment has been shown to play a pivotal role in Aβ misfolding and aggregation [53]. The hydroxyl group, methoxy group, and phenyl ring of guaiacol in ferulic aldehyde and vanillin could effectively interact with the aromatic residues 19FF20 in the hot spot (16KLVFFA21) of the CHC region through the formation of hydrogen bonds and π–π stacking [51], supporting that the highly dense guaiacol moieties on Cur-CQDs-210 can strongly inhibit the Aβ association.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cur-CQDs suppressed hyperphosphorylation of tau protein
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cells was not significantly affected by Cur-CQDs even at a high concentration of 200 µg mL−1 ( Fig. 4a), whereas 12.5 µg mL−1 of curcumin could affect the cell viability. Results suggested that the pyrolysis of curcumin into CQDs could attenuate the toxicity of curcumin. The cytotoxic mechanism of curcumin was multifaceted, targeting several cellular pathways involved in the induction of apoptosis and cell cycle arrest. Curcumin has been shown to trigger programmed cell death (apoptosis) in various cancer cell types by activating both the intrinsic and extrinsic apoptotic pathways such as the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), regulation of Bcl-2 family proteins, caspases, and the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria [54]. Curcumin can also arrest the cell cycle at different phases by modulation of cyclins, cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs), and CDK inhibitors [55]. In contrast to the induced production of ROS, our previous work demonstrated that Cur-CQDs could suppress the intracellular ROS generation induced by enterovirus 71 [33].

Tau hyperphosphorylation, a key characteristic of AD, was studied using OA to induce this process in order to examine the suppressive effects of Cur-CQDs [56]. The presence of hyperphosphorylated tau protein, relative to tau 46, increased with OA treatment. When pre-incubated with 100 µg mL−1 of Cur-CQDs, particularly Cur-CQDs-210, the hyperphosphorylation of tau induced by OA was significantly decreased (Fig. 4b−d). Although previous studies have shown curcumin's ability to inhibit tau hyperphosphorylation [31], there have been no reports on the impact of CDs on tau phosphorylation. Cur-CQDs-210 demonstrated not only exceptional biocompatibility but also significant inhibitory capacity against tau hyperphosphorylation.

Although the exact mechanism by which curcumin inhibited tau hyperphosphorylation was not fully understood, several hypotheses have been proposed [28,57,58]. Hyperphosphorylation of tau protein was regulated by several kinases, such as glycogen synthase kinase-3β (GSK-3β) and cyclin-dependent kinase 5 (CDK5) [57]. Curcumin was thought to inhibit the activities of these kinases, thereby reducing tau hyperphosphorylation [28]. Curcumin may also activate phosphatases such as protein phosphatase 2A (PP2A) to promote the dephosphorylation of tau protein thus reducing hyperphosphorylation [58]. Oxidative stress was known to contribute to the hyperphosphorylation of tau proteins. Curcumin possesses antioxidant properties at low concentrations, which may help protect against oxidative stress and reduce the hyperphosphorylation of tau proteins [59]. In addition, curcumin can suppress inflammation and subsequently decrease tau hyperphosphorylation. Curcumin has been shown to modulate various signaling pathways, including the PI3K/Akt and Wnt/β-catenin pathways, to impact the phosphorylation status of tau protein and reduce hyperphosphorylation [60]. After the pyrolysis of curcumin into Cur-CQDs, we believe that the functional groups retained in the CQDs may exert their effects through these proposed mechanisms. These mechanisms may work in combination or synergistically to exert the effects of Cur-CQDs on tau hyperphosphorylation, which needs to be investigated in the future.

Overcoming the blood-brain barrier (BBB) poses a critical challenge in the treatment of AD. QDs have demonstrated promising potential in addressing this challenge, as evidenced by various formulations successfully traversing the BBB, including tryptophan-based carbon dots [61], glucose-derived CDs [62], and nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [63]. The capacity of Cur-CQDs for BBB penetration warrants further investigation. Additionally, there remains a need to explore several questions and aspects to obtain a more comprehensive understanding of the underlying mechanisms and potential applications of Cur-CQDs in AD treatment. Specific molecular interactions between the anisole and guaiacol moieties on the Cur-CQDs' surface and Aβ42 monomers needs to be investigated, which could elucidate the exact mechanisms responsible for the inhibition of aggregation. Additional in vitro and in vivo studies need to be performed to confirm the observed effects of Cur-CQDs on Aβ aggregation and tau hyperphosphorylation and to determine the optimal dosage and treatment duration for maximum efficacy. Potential synergistic effects of Cur-CQDs in combination with other established or experimental AD therapies should be explored, which could lead to the development of more effective multi-target treatment strategies. Further research into the safety and biocompatibility of Cur-CQDs for long-term use needs to be conducted, as well as their potential side effects, to ensure the development of safe and effective therapies for AD patients. Overall, the present study provides promising preliminary evidence for the potential use of Cur-CQDs, particularly Cur-CQDs-210, in AD treatment. However, more extensive research is necessary to fully understand the mechanisms of action and to establish the most effective therapeutic strategies using Cur-CQDs.

The present study showed valuable findings on the impact of curcumin pyrolysis into Cur-CQDs and their potential role in regulating Aβ aggregation and tau protein hyperphosphorylation. Among the prepared Cur-CQDs, Cur-CQDs-210 was the most effective one in inhibiting Aβ42 monomer aggregation. This may be attributed to the abundant anisole and guaiacol moieties on the surface of the Cur-CQDs, which could be interacting with the Aβ42 monomers to prevent aggregation. The conversion of curcumin into Cur-CQDs through pyrolysis also resulted in reduced cytotoxicity and increased solubility, which were significant advantages for potential therapeutic applications. These properties allowed for the use of higher concentrations without causing harm to cells, which could improve the effectiveness of treatments. A notable highlight of our study is that it is the first report discussing the amelioration of tau hyperphosphorylation by CDs, specifically Cur-CQDs-210. This finding opened up new avenues for research into the use of CDs as a potential therapeutic agent for AD.

Jie Lay Lim: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Chin-Jung Lin: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Chih-Ching Huang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Lin-Chau Chang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
